jsonLdWeb.name: Profit Margin Calculator
jsonLdWeb.description: Calculate profit margin from revenue and cost. See gross margin, net margin, markup percentage, and total profit.

acceptedAnswer.text: Profit margin is the percentage of revenue that remains as profit after costs are deducted. It is calculated as (Revenue - Cost) / Revenue × 100. A 30% margin means 30 cents of every dollar earned is profit.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between gross margin and net margin?

acceptedAnswer.text: Gross margin only considers the direct cost of goods sold (COGS). Net margin accounts for all expenses including COGS, operating expenses, taxes, and interest. Net margin is always lower than gross margin and gives a more complete picture of profitability.

mainEntity.name: How do you calculate profit margin?

acceptedAnswer.text: Profit Margin = (Revenue - Cost) / Revenue × 100. For example, if revenue is $100 and cost is $60, margin = ($100 - $60) / $100 × 100 = 40%. This means 40% of each sale is profit.

mainEntity.name: What is a good profit margin?

acceptedAnswer.text: It varies by industry. Software/SaaS: 70-90% gross margin. Consulting: 50-80%. Retail: 20-50%. Restaurants: 3-9% net margin. Manufacturing: 10-30%. A margin above the industry average is generally considered good.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between margin and markup?

acceptedAnswer.text: Margin is profit as a percentage of selling price. Markup is profit as a percentage of cost. If you sell something for $100 that cost $60, margin is 40% but markup is 66.7%. Markup is always higher than margin for the same transaction.

mainEntity.name: How do I calculate revenue from margin and cost?

acceptedAnswer.text: Revenue = Cost / (1 - Margin/100). For example, if cost is $60 and desired margin is 40%, revenue = $60 / (1 - 0.40) = $60 / 0.60 = $100.

howItWorks:
How to Calculate Profit Margin

Profit margin measures how much of each dollar of revenue a business keeps as profit. It is one of the most important metrics for evaluating business health.

Margin % = (Revenue − Cost) / Revenue × 100

Two types of margin:

  • Gross Margin = (Revenue − COGS) / Revenue — only direct costs
  • Net Margin = (Revenue − All Expenses) / Revenue — includes overhead, taxes, etc.
  • Markup = (Revenue − Cost) / Cost × 100 — profit relative to cost, not price

Example: Revenue $100,000 − Cost $60,000 = Profit $40,000 → 40% margin, 66.7% markup
faqHTML:
Frequently Asked Questions

What is profit margin?

Profit margin is the percentage of revenue that remains as profit after costs are deducted. A 40% profit margin means that for every dollar of revenue, $0.40 is profit. It is calculated as (Revenue − Cost) / Revenue × 100.

What is the difference between gross margin and net margin?

Gross margin only subtracts the direct cost of goods sold (COGS) from revenue. Net margin subtracts all expenses — including operating costs, taxes, interest, and overhead. Net margin is always lower than gross margin and gives the most complete picture of actual profitability.

How do you calculate profit margin?

Profit Margin = (Revenue − Cost) / Revenue × 100. For example, if revenue is $100,000 and cost is $60,000, the margin is ($100,000 − $60,000) / $100,000 × 100 = 40%.

What is a good profit margin?

It varies significantly by industry. Software companies often see 70–90% gross margins. Retail typically runs 25–50% gross. Restaurants average just 3–9% net margin. Any margin above your industry average is generally considered good. Improving margin over time is the key indicator of business health.

What is the difference between margin and markup?

Margin is profit as a percentage of the selling price (revenue). Markup is profit as a percentage of cost. Selling something for $100 that cost $60 gives a 40% margin but a 66.7% markup. Markup is always a higher number than margin for the same transaction.

How do I calculate revenue from margin and cost?

Revenue = Cost / (1 − Margin/100). For example, if cost is $60,000 and desired margin is 40%, revenue = $60,000 / (1 − 0.40) = $60,000 / 0.60 = $100,000.
